ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Turkish pide. FOODON_00002765 (flatbread food product) is the closest defensible parent category covering this meat-topped flatbread.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for pide. A0CKW (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible parent. Lamb topping is not captured at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Turkish pide or lamb pide specifically.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole lamb pide, a boat-shaped flatbread approximately 40–50 cm long, typically served as a single-person main course weighing around 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb mince pide — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Ground lamb, raw (FDC ID 174381) and Bread, pita, white, enriched (FDC ID 174924), weighted 50:50 by edible mass
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for lamb pide as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for ground lamb (raw, FDC 174381) and white pita bread (FDC 174924) in an approximate 50:50 ratio by edible weight, reflecting a typical lamb pide composition (roughly equal parts dough and lamb topping). Cooking losses for lamb (~25% moisture loss on baking) have been applied. This is a medium-confidence estimate. A direct laboratory analysis or HPB/ICMR composite dish entry would supersede this estimate.

gi_source.title: Pita bread, white — GI value 57 (glucose reference); analogous estimate applied to lamb pide dough component
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamb pide as a composite dish. The GI of 57 is taken from white pita bread (University of Sydney GI Database, glucose scale), which is the closest approved-source analogue for the pide dough base. The lamb topping contributes negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (57 × 67.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 38.5, rounded to 38.6. This is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement of lamb pide would supersede this value.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~930 mg per full 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A full lamb pide serving provides approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and avoidance of additional high-sodium condiments such as pickles or salty sauces is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 57) and high glycemic load per full serving (~38.6)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI of the pide dough and the substantial carbohydrate load (~67.5 g per 300 g serving) can produce a significant postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a pide, pair with a salad or non-starchy vegetables, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ground lamb (~3.8 g per 100 g; ~11.4 g per full serving) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from ground lamb contributes to LDL cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consider requesting leaner lamb mince, limiting serving size to half a pide, and balancing the meal with vegetables to reduce overall saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb pide (Turkish: kuzu pidesi) is a traditional Turkish flatbread dish consisting of a hand-shaped, boat-like leavened wheat dough base topped with a seasoned ground lamb mixture and baked in a wood-fired or conventional oven at high heat. The dough is soft and slightly chewy, with a lightly crisped underside, while the lamb topping is savoury, aromatic, and moist, seasoned with cumin, paprika, black pepper, onion, tomato, and fresh parsley. Pide is a staple of Turkish cuisine and is often described as a Turkish pizza or Turkish flatbread, though it is distinct in shape, dough texture, and topping style. A full pide is typically 40–50 cm long and served as a single-person main course. Nutritionally, lamb pide is a moderate-energy, protein-rich dish providing approximately 242 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (~14.8 g/100 g) from the lamb and a moderate carbohydrate contribution (~22.5 g/100 g) from the dough. The dish contains moderate saturated fat (~3.8 g/100 g) and sodium (~310 mg/100 g), making portion awareness important for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (estimated GI 57, analogous to white pita) and high glycemic load per full serving (~38.6) are clinically relevant for blood glucose management. Lamb pide is naturally halal and dairy-free but contains gluten. It is commonly served with fresh salad, lemon wedges, and chilli flakes in Turkish restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lamb pide?
What is the difference between pide and pizza?
How much protein is in lamb pide?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for lamb pide as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for ground lamb and white pita bread in an approximate 50:50 ratio. GI is estimated from the closest approved-source analogue (white pita bread, University of Sydney GI Database). Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (ground lamb FDC 174381 and white pita bread FDC 174924) in an approximate 50:50 ratio by edible weight; a direct laboratory analysis or approved composite dish entry would supersede these values. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for the components used in this composite estimate. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for the components used in this composite estimate. 4. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on white pita bread (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for lamb pide exists in an approved source. 5. The food_name has been normalised from the menu-style input '84. Lamb Pidde' to the canonical English form 'Lamb Pide'; 'Lamb Pidde' is retained as an alias reflecting the field survey data menu spelling. 6. Turkish cuisine does not fall under the Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese mandatory multilingual naming rules; therefore Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not required and are set to null. The romanized Turkish name 'Kuzu Pidesi' is provided. 7. Halal status is marked true as lamb pide prepared in a Turkish restaurant context is conventionally halal; this should be verified against the specific restaurant's certification.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalised food_name from '84. Lamb Pidde' to canonical 'Lamb Pide'; retained 'Lamb Pidde' and 'pidde lamb' as aliases.
Iteration 1: Constructed composite nutrient estimate from USDA FoodData Central ground lamb (FDC 174381) and white pita bread (FDC 174924) at 50:50 ratio with cooking loss adjustment.
Iteration 1: Applied GI 57 from University of Sydney GI Database (white pita bread analogue) and calculated GL per 300 g serving.
Iteration 1: Confirmed Turkish cuisine does not trigger Singaporean multilingual naming requirements; set Chinese/Malay/Tamil/Hindi local_names to null; provided romanized Turkish name.
Iteration 1: Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) in notes.

Iteration 2: Verified aliases_by_language consistency — all strings in aliases_by_language confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases list. Confirmed ontology match_type values use only allowed terms (exact, closest_parent, none).
